content.full_text:
U1

RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 19, 1995

Judith Allen
Public Utilities Counsel
Public Utilities Commission
505 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, CA  94102-3298

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-188

Dear Ms. Allen:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Daniel Fessler regarding his responsibilities as
president of the California Public Utilities Commission under the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1.  May President Fessler accept the travel reimbursement
from either the National Association of Regulatory Utility
Commissioners or the Nuclear Electric Insurance Limited?

2.  Are the travel payments characterized as income, gifts,
or honoraria?

___________________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
Regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

128 J STREET, SUITE 500, SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 95814     PHONE 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-188
Page 2


3. Would President Fessler have a conflict of interest with
respect to the donor organization or businesses that are members
of the donor organization?

CONCLUSIONS

1 and 2. If President Fessler does not provide consideration
of equal or greater value to the source of the travel payment (as
discussed below), the president will be receiving a gift that is
subject to the $280 gift limit of the Act. If the president does
provide consideration of equal or greater value, the payments will
be considered income.

2. In either event, President Fessler will have a conflict
of interest with respect to decisions affecting the source of the
payment, whether it be the National Association of Regulatory
Utility Commissioners or the Nuclear Electric Insurance Limited.
The president would not have a conflict of interest with respect
to the individual members of the organizations.

FACTS

President Fessler is president of the California Public
Utilities Commission and is also a member of the National
Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners (the NARUC). The
NARUC is an association of state public utilities commissioners
formed as a nonprofit organization pursuant to Internal Revenue
Code Section 501(c)(4).

The NARUC has appointed President Fessler to the advisory
council of the Nuclear Electric Insurance Limited (the NEIL). The
NEIL is a mutual insurance company organized to provide additional
insurance to the power industry because of liability related to
nuclear generating units. Three California energy companies have
an interest in the NEIL, Pacific Gas and Electric Company has a
voting interest of 1.6 percent, San Diego Gas and Electric Company
has a voting interest of 5 percent, and Southern California Edison
has a voting interest of 2.5 percent. The remaining 90.9 percent
of the NEIL is controlled by utilities that are not regulated by
the PUC.

As a representative of the NARUC, President Fessler must
attend the annual board meeting and general meeting of the NEIL on
June 26 and 27, 1995, in Bermuda. The president will provide
oversight on behalf of the NARUC, including observing meetings and
reporting back to the NARUC. The president, as the NARUC
representative, may also react to discussions at the meetings, but
does not have a right to vote at the meetings. You stated that
the NEIL will pay the president's travel expenses.

Our File No. A-95-188
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Honoraria

An honorarium is defined as any payment made in
consideration for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering. (Section 89502.)
The president will not be making a speech or writing an article,
nor is he being paid to "attend" the event, as defined in
Regulation 18931.3. Regulation 18931.3 defines "attendance" as
being present during, making an appearance at, or serving as host
or master of ceremonies for, any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, or like gathering. This
definition contemplates more than being paid to observe and report
back to the NARUC.

Income and Gifts

Section 82028 provides:

(a) "Gift" means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not
received and includes a rebate or discount in the
price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business
to members of the public without regard to official
status.

Absent an exception, the payments for travel received from
the NARUC would be a gift unless consideration of equal or greater
value is provided in exchange for the payments.

If consideration of equal or greater value is provided to the
source of the payment, the payment would be considered income
rather than a gift. While we have no exact formula to determine
whether consideration of equal or greater value has been provided
by an official, the following general guidelines may be assistance.
The value of services rendered may be proved by evidence as to the
customary rate of compensation for such services, irrespective of official status. (Passi v. Tassi (1958)
160 Cal.App.2d 680, 690-691.) Also relevant in the determination
might be the length of time spent rendering the services, or
whether the services are of the type not readily available from
others.

Our File No. A-95-188
Page 4


     Ultimately, however, the determination of whether equal
consideration has been provided is necessarily a factual one.2

     Consequently:

     1.  If President Fessler provides consideration of equal or
greater value to the NARUC, then the payments would be income.3

     We note that while the NEIL is the actual donor of the
travel, we assume that the NARUC controls who used the travel.
The NEIL would presumably provide the payments no matter who was
acting as the NARUC representative.  Thus, in determining whether
the payments are income or gifts, it would appear that the NARUC
is the actual donor.

     This conclusion might be different if the NEIL generally did
not pay for travel and accommodations, but paid for the
president's travel and accommodations because it was President Fessler who would be
attending.  If this is the case, you should contact us for further
advice.  Under such circumstances, both the NEIL and the NARUC
might be considered the source of the payment in question.4

     2.  If consideration of equal or greater value was not
provided to the NARUC, the payments would be a gift, and the rules
discussed below would apply.

Gift Limits

     Generally, free transportation, lodging, and subsistence
provided to a state official in connection with delivering a
speech or participating in a panel discussion concerning issues of


2    If an official claims that the payment or payments are income
and not a gift, the official has the burden of providing that the
consideration provided was of equal or greater value than the
payment or payments received.  (Section 82028.)

3    Section 82030(b)(2) exempts "reimbursement for travel expenses
and per diem received from a bona fide educational, academic, or
charitable organization."  Consistent with the Internal Revenue
Code, we have interpreted "bona fide educational, academic, or
charitable organization" as those organizations formed under
Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3).  This would not apply to
a nonprofit civic organization such as the NARUC.  (See, Abt
Advice Letter, No. I-91-436; Morton Advice Letter, No. A-95-138.)

4    However, even if the NEIL was the source of the payment, the
Commission will generally not pierce through an organization to
the members of the organization unless the members in question had
a significant or controlling interest in the organization.  (See,
Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-069.)  Under your facts, the
utilities in question have relatively small interests in the NEIL.

Our File No. A-95-188
Page 5


public policy are considered to be gifts or honoraria.  (See
Regulation 18950.1.)

However, Section 89505(b) states that where a public official
receives a gift of travel, Section 89506 controls whether the gift
is subject to the new gift limit.  Section 89506 provides:

     (a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

          (1) The travel is in connection with a
speech given by the elected state officer,
member of a state board or commission, or
designated employee of a state agency, the
lodging and subsistence expenses are limited
to the day immediately preceding, the day of,
and the day immediately following the speech,
and the travel is within the United States.

          (2) The travel is provided by a
government, a governmental agency, a foreign
government, a governmental authority, a bona
fide public or private educational
institution, as defined in Section 203 of the
Revenue and Taxation Code, or a nonprofit
charitable or religious organization which is
exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3)
of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person
domiciled outside the United States which
substantially satisfies the requirements for
tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of
the Internal Revenue Code.

                    Section 89506(a)(2).

While participation in the conference may be reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue
of state, national, or international public policy, the NARUC is
not a bona fide public or private educational institution as
defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, or an
organization formed under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3).
Thus, this exception would not apply and if full and adequate
consideration is not provided by the president, the gift limit of
$280 per calendar year would apply.

Conflicts of Interest; Disclosure and Disqualification

Pursuant to the Act, every public official must disclose all
his or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected

Our File No. A-95-188
Page 6


by the exercise of the official's duties.  (Sections 81002(c),
87200-87313.)  In addition, Section 87100 prohibits any public
official at any level of state or local government from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows or has reason to know the official has a
financial interest.5  (Section 87100.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on (among others) any
source of gifts of $280 or more, or any source of income of $250
or more to the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.  (Section 87103(c) and (e).)

Consequently, if the NARUC is a source of income of $250 or
more, or of gifts of $280 or more, they would need to be disclosed
and the president would be required to disqualify himself from any
decision which would have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on the NARUC.6  You have not asked any questions about a
specific decision before the PUC.  However, we have enclosed some
general materials for your information.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.7

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division


_____________________
5    The disqualification requirement of the Act is a separate
obligation that is not affected by public disclosure of a conflict
of interest.

6    As noted above, your facts do not suggest that the members of
either organization are the actual source of gifts to them.  Thus, the
conflict of interest provisions would not apply to decisions
affecting the members.

7    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
